title: Deadlift 1RM Calculator (Epley) | Hacé Cuentas
description: Estimate your deadlift one-rep max using the Epley formula. Free, fast, and accurate.
intro: The Deadlift 1RM Estimator calculates your **one-repetition maximum (1RM)** — the heaviest single lift you can complete with proper form — using the **Epley formula**: `1RM = weight × (1 + reps/30)`. Enter the load you lifted and how many reps you completed (ideally 2–10), and the calculator instantly projects your theoretical max. This is the go-to method for powerlifters, strength coaches, and gym athletes who want to program training zones (e.g., 70% 1RM for hypertrophy, 90%+ for max strength) without risking injury on an all-out attempt. The Epley equation is one of the oldest and most widely validated 1RM prediction models, originally published in 1985 and consistently benchmarked against direct testing in peer-reviewed literature.
keyTakeaway: **1RM = Weight (kg) × (1 + Reps ÷ 30)** — e.g., 100 kg × 8 reps → 1RM ≈ 127 kg; keep reps between 2–10 for best accuracy (error < 5%).
useCases: Setting deadlift training zones: once you have your estimated 1RM, load 70–75% for 3×10 hypertrophy work, 80–85% for 3×5 strength work, and 90–95% for heavy singles — all without ever grinding a true max attempt.

Tracking strength progress over a training block: lift 120 kg × 6 reps in week 1 (est. 1RM = 144 kg) and 120 kg × 9 reps in week 8 (est. 1RM = 156 kg) to quantify a 12 kg improvement without retesting your max each cycle.

Comparing performance to bodyweight strength standards: after estimating your 1RM, divide it by your bodyweight to get a strength ratio and check it against published deadlift classification tables (e.g., an 'Elite' male lifter pulls ≥3.0× bodyweight, per Lon Kilgore's standards).

Pre-competition peaking: powerlifters use their estimated 1RM in the final 2–3 weeks to select opening attempts on the platform — typically 90–92% of the estimated max — reducing the risk of bombing out on attempt 1.

fields.label: Weight (kg)
fields.help: Weight of the load lifted (bar and plates) in kg.

explanation:
## How It's Calculated

The **Epley formula** (1985) is the standard for estimating 1RM from a submaximal set:

```
1RM = Weight × (1 + Reps / 30)
```

**Step-by-step example:**
- You lift **80 kg for 5 reps**
- 1RM = 80 × (1 + 5/30)
- 1RM = 80 × (1 + 0.1667)
- 1RM = 80 × 1.1667
- **1RM ≈ 93.3 kg → rounded to 93 kg**

> ⚠️ **Accuracy window:** The Epley formula is most reliable for **2–10 reps**. At 1 rep it returns the exact input weight (correct). Above 10 reps, fatigue compounds and the model increasingly overestimates true maximal strength.

---

## Reference Table — Epley Multipliers by Rep Count

| Reps | Epley Multiplier | % of 1RM | Example: 100 kg set |
|------|-----------------|----------|---------------------|
| 1    | 1.033           | 100%     | 100 kg              |
| 2    | 1.067           | 97%      | 107 kg              |
| 3    | 1.100           | 94%      | 110 kg              |
| 4    | 1.133           | 92%      | 113 kg              |
| 5    | 1.167           | 89%      | 117 kg              |
| 6    | 1.200           | 86%      | 120 kg              |
| 7    | 1.233           | 84%      | 123 kg              |
| 8    | 1.267           | 82%      | 127 kg              |
| 10   | 1.333           | 77%      | 133 kg              |
| 12   | 1.400           | 74%      | 140 kg *(less reliable)* |

---

## Typical Use Cases with Real Numbers

### Case 1 — Intermediate lifter programming a strength block
- **Input:** 100 kg × 6 reps
- **Estimated 1RM:** 100 × (1 + 6/30) = 100 × 1.2 = **120 kg**
- **Training zones derived:**
  - Heavy (90%): 108 kg — used for 2–3 singles
  - Strength (82.5%): 99 kg — used for 3×4
  - Hypertrophy (72.5%): 87 kg — used for 4×8

### Case 2 — Tracking progress over 8 weeks
- Week 1: 140 kg × 4 reps → 1RM = 140 × 1.133 = **158.6 kg**
- Week 8: 140 kg × 7 reps → 1RM = 140 × 1.233 = **172.7 kg**
- **Net gain: +14.1 kg estimated 1RM** without a single all-out max attempt

### Case 3 — Competition attempt selection
- Athlete estimates 1RM = **220 kg**
- Opening attempt: 220 × 0.91 = **200 kg** (safe, almost certain success)
- Second attempt: 220 × 0.96 = **211 kg**
- Third attempt: 220 × 1.00 = **220 kg** (full projected max)

---

## Common Errors

1. **Using more than 10 reps as input.** At 15+ reps, the Epley formula may overestimate your true 1RM by 10–15% because it doesn't fully account for cardiovascular fatigue or fast-twitch fiber depletion at high rep ranges. Stick to sets of 2–8 for best precision.

2. **Lifting to failure vs. stopping with reps in reserve (RIR).** If your set of 5 reps was *grinding failure*, the estimate is valid. If you stopped at rep 5 with 3 reps left in the tank, your estimated 1RM will be significantly underestimated. Always note whether the set was near-maximal.

3. **Ignoring rest and fatigue state.** Performing a test set after a heavy warm-up or in a fatigued state produces a lower rep count, artificially deflating the 1RM estimate. Use the calculator after a proper warm-up, on a fresh training day.

4. **Confusing estimated 1RM with competition-ready max.** The Epley formula predicts a *physiological* ceiling under ideal conditions. Real competition 1RMs involve psychological readiness, equipment (belt, deadlift bar flex, chalk), and peaking — factors the formula cannot capture.

5. **Not accounting for lift specificity.** A 1RM estimated from a sumo-stance deadlift set is not directly transferable to conventional deadlift maxes, and vice versa. Always test and estimate within the same movement pattern.

faq.q: How accurate is the Epley formula for deadlifts?
faq.a: Research published in the *Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research* reports that Epley's formula has a mean error of approximately 3–5% when tested sets are performed in the 2–10 rep range. Accuracy drops notably above 10 reps, where overestimation of 8–15% is common. For most practical programming purposes, a ±5 kg margin is considered acceptable.

faq.q: Is the Epley formula better than Brzycki, Lander, or Lombardi for deadlifts?
faq.a: Multiple comparison studies (e.g., Mayhew et al., 1992; Chapman et al., 1998) show that Epley, Brzycki, and Lander produce nearly identical estimates for reps ≤6, diverging by 1–3% at higher rep counts. Epley tends to slightly overestimate at 8–10 reps, while Brzycki slightly underestimates. For deadlifts specifically, Epley is the most widely used in coaching software and apps.

faq.q: Should I test my 1RM directly instead of estimating?
faq.a: A direct 1RM test is more accurate but carries higher injury risk — especially in the deadlift, where spinal loading at maximal weights is significant. The NIH and major strength organizations recommend estimated 1RM testing for recreational athletes, novices, and during high-volume training blocks, reserving true 1RM attempts for competition or formal peaking cycles only.

faq.q: What rep range gives the most accurate 1RM estimate?
faq.a: The sweet spot is **3–6 reps** at approximately 85–90% of your true max. At this range, Epley's error is typically below 3%. Sets of 1–2 reps are too close to the actual max to provide meaningful extrapolation, and sets of 10+ introduce excessive metabolic fatigue that corrupts the mechanical prediction model.

faq.q: How do I use my estimated 1RM to set up a deadlift program?
faq.a: Standard percentage-based programming (used in systems like 5/3/1 by Jim Wendler and Texas Method) prescribes: 65–70% for volume/technique work, 75–85% for strength-building sets, and 90–95%+ for peak intensity. Multiply your estimated 1RM by these percentages to get exact working weights. For example, a 180 kg estimated 1RM → 85% = 153 kg as a top working set.

faq.q: Does bodyweight affect the interpretation of my deadlift 1RM?
faq.a: Yes. Strength standards are typically expressed as a **bodyweight ratio**. According to widely cited classifications (Kilgore, Rippetoe & Kilgore, *Practical Programming*): Untrained: 1.0× BW; Novice: 1.5× BW; Intermediate: 2.0× BW; Advanced: 2.5× BW; Elite: 3.0× BW (male). For a 90 kg athlete, an estimated 1RM of 180 kg = 2.0× BW (Intermediate level).

faq.q: Does the Epley formula work the same for women?
faq.a: Yes — the Epley formula is sex-neutral; it predicts the mechanical 1RM based on load and reps regardless of sex. However, absolute strength standards differ. Female deadlift classifications are roughly 25–30% lower in absolute terms: Elite women typically pull ≥2.0–2.2× bodyweight. The formula itself requires no modification, only the reference tables for interpreting results change.

faq.q: Can I use pounds (lbs) instead of kilograms in the formula?
faq.a: Yes. The Epley formula is unit-agnostic — the mathematical relationship `1RM = W × (1 + reps/30)` works identically in lbs or kg. Simply enter your weight in lbs and the result will also be in lbs. To convert: 1 kg ≈ 2.2046 lbs. For example, 185 lbs × 5 reps → 1RM = 185 × 1.167 = **215.9 lbs ≈ 98 kg**.

sources.name: NIH National Library of Medicine — Prediction of One Repetition Maximum Strength from Multiple Repetition Maximum Testing (PubMed)

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, no external data updates required.
